sutta: [Music] now 2 brahmins skilled in metaphysics or the philosophy of mine visited the exalted 1 and after greeting him and exchanging the usual compliments sat down at 1 side so seated they said this to him master gotama purana kasapa all knowing all seeing professors unlimited knowledge and vision [Music] saying whether i walk stand sleep or wait always ever knowledge and vision are present and he has thus declared with infinite knowledge i abide knowing seeing a finite world master gotama the all knowing all seeing also professes unlimited knowledge and vision in like words but he has declared thus with infinite knowledge i abide knowing seeing an infinite world of these 2 talkers on knowledge master gotama of these 2 who contradict each other which of the 2 speaks the truth which lies and the buddha said enough brahmanas which speaks the truth which lies let it be i will teach you dhamma brahmins listen i will speak i just stopped here for a while these 2 external ascetic leaders mentioned here purana kasapa and niganta nataputa the jain they are 2 of 6 well-known as uh external sex teachers during the time of the buddha and purana kasapa said that he has infinite knowledge knowing and seeing a finite world whereas niganta nataputa says that he has infinite knowledge knowing and seeing an infinite world so these 2 brahmins and they they ask the buddha once is that the world is finite the other 1 says that the world is infinite which means that they contradict each other so the brahmins wanted to know which 1 was speaking the truth and which 1 was lying but the buddha instead of telling them plainly that the 2 of them were lying the buddha decided to just ignore the questions and teach them dhamma because if he spoke the truth and said that the 2 are lying these 2 brahmanas may not believe him because they are not actually disciples of the buddha yet and why are they lying because they are saying that whether they walk stand sleep or awaken always all the time knowledge and vision are present in them but in another suta the buddha has said that this is not possible it is not possible for a being for a person to know everything all the time even the buddha himself he only knows when he contemplates when he puts attention to what he wants to know then he knows if he does not want to know for example much of the time the buddha spends in meditation [Music] in deep meditation stage and a lot of things that goes on in the world he does not know because he's not much bothered to know and because of that sometimes quite often they must have to come and inform the buddha for example that devadatta 1 of his former disciples is planning to kill him or something so the suta continues yes sir they replied that means the brahmins replied and the exalted 1 said imagine brahmins 4 men standing at the 4 corners of the world each endowed with supreme pace and speed the supreme length of stride as an archer mighty with ball skilled adapt marksman may wing with ease a slender shaft across a palm tree's shadow let such be the speed as western sea from eastern sea such their stride now suppose the man standing at the eastern corner were to say by walking i'll reach the end of the world no man spend lifespan over a 100 years and he lived 100 years and walked for a 100 years except when eating drinking chewing munching answering nature's calls and dispelling fatigue by sleep he would die before he reached the end of the world and suppose a man at the western corner at the northern corner and the man at the southern corner were to say likewise though each walked for a 100 years except when eating and so forth they would die before they reach the end of the world and why not brah means by such journeys do i declare that the world's end may be known seen reached yet i declare brahmins that without reaching the end of the world there is no ending of ill i stop here for a moment the buddha is saying i suppose 4 persons they can walk with extreme at an extremely fast speed and their strides if they took 1 step now they could go from the western sea to the eastern sea such great is the strident and the speed like uh arrow shot by a expert archer very fast yet if they kept walking for 100 years they would not be able to reach the end of the world why because the world their space has no limits you can't find the boundaries of space that's why they keep on walking or flying they can never reach the end of the world there is a similar suta called i think we have come across that suta before i think under the book of the force it is also found in the samyutta nikaya where a deva came to ask the buddha whether by flying he could get out of the world reach the end of the world and the buddha said it is not possible so the buddha continues brahmins these 5 strands of sense desire are called the world in the code of the aryan what 5 shapes cognized by the eye longed for alluring pleasurable lovely bound up with passion and desire sounds cognized by the ear smells by the nose taste by the tongue contacts cognized by the touch long for alluring pleasurable lovely bound up with passion and desire these are the 5 strands of sense desire brahmins i stop here for a moment why are the 5 strengths of sun's desire called the world because if we examine carefully where is the world we would have to say we know a world exists because of seeing the forms in the world hearing the sounds in the world smelling the smells in the world tasting the taste in the world and touching the things in the world contacts in the world so because of these 5 senses the world arises to us without the 5 senses there is no world that is why the 5 strengths of sense desire are called the world in the aryan corner and the buddha continues now consider brahmanas the monk who allu from sen's desire enters and abides in the first jhana he is said to have come to the world's end and abide at the world's end and some say thus of him this 1 is still world bound still not gone out from the world i too say this of him he is still world bound still not gone out from the world then consider brahmins the monk who enters and abides in the second jhana the third the fourth jhana the sphere of infinite space the sphere of infinite consciousness the sphere of nothingness is fear of neither perception or non-perception in each case he is said to have come to the world's end and abide there and some say of him as before and i too say of him he is still world bound still not gone out from the world now consider brahmins the monk who passing wholly beyond the sphere of neither perception nor non-perception enters and abides in this ending of perception and feeling and by wisdom sees that the asavas are completely destroyed that mong is said to have come to the worlds and and abide at the worlds and to have passed through the world's entanglement that's the end of the sutta
commentary: so here the buddha is saying when among enters and abides in the jhanas he comes to the worlds and can abide at the world's end but even though he comes to the world's end he has still not gone out from the world why is why is it that he has he said to come to the world and because when the monk enters the janus the world recedes from him the world of forms sounds smells taste touch these received from him he is no more conscious of them that's why he is come to the end of the world but however he has still not gone out from the world he has still not gone out from the world it is only when he attains the sphere of the ending of perception and feeling when the sixth normal consciousness stops then the he uh uh and when the asavas are destroyed then he has not only come to the world and he has gone out of the world he is no more bound by the world so that's the end of the shutdown so from here you can see here that the uh [Music] samatha meditation is very important tranquilization of the mind because to get out of the world we have to practice samata even before the getting out of the world to come to the end of the world to abide at the world's end we have to attain the jaanas so samata practice the meditation samata meditation is very much the buddha's teachings it is like this where it's very clear that if we want to come to the world's end we must attain the janas we attain the jhanas we come to the worlds and a little bit more effort and and with the samatha practice plus vipassana then we get out of the world so samatha and vipassana they go hand in hand you cannot separate the samatha from the persona practice both are required in the aryan eightfold path as the seventh and the eighth factor seventh is the right mindfulness which is vipassana practice and the eighth is white concentration which is samatha practice for attaining the jhanas the nexuta is
